content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Self-Reflection Prompts to Attenuate Social Biases in LLM Outputs",
  "problem": "Large language models frequently generate outputs that reflect or amplify harmful social biases and stereotypes. Existing prompting methods to reduce these biases often degrade task performance or lack generalizability.",
  "motivation": "Mitigating social bias in LLMs is crucial as these models are increasingly deployed in sensitive, real-world contexts, including education, hiring, and counseling. Prompt-based debiasing is attractive due to its model-agnostic, low-resource nature and rapid deployability—yet current methods are insufficient against subtle or emergent biases.",
  "method": "We propose Counterfactual Self-Reflection Prompting (CSRP), which augments user queries with an automatically generated counterfactual ('What would your answer be if the subject belonged to a different demographic group?') and a self-reflection meta-prompt ('Does your answer risk perpetuating stereotypes? If so, revise.'). CSRP is implemented as a structured, two-stage prompt: (1) the model generates an initial response, (2) then the model is prompted to reflect on potential bias and revise its output, referencing the counterfactual scenario. This mechanism is fully automated and operates at the prompt level, requiring no LLM fine-tuning.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CSRP on the BOLD and StereoSet benchmarks, comparing against the strongest prompt-based debiasing baseline: Chain-of-Thought with explicit bias warnings. Metrics include bias scores (e.g., Stereotype, Association, Language Modeling scores) and task accuracy. The key experiment will test if CSRP reduces bias without significant degradation of core task performance."
}
